TargetRLF1: Fa Kɔndɔm Di Dwuma: Fa kɔndɔm di dwuma yiye bere nyinaa na fa di dwuma bere nyinaa bere a wode wo ho hyɛ ɔbea awotwaa mu, akyi berɛmo mu, ne anom nna mu. To Nna mu Ahokafo ano hye: Tew wɔn a wɔne wɔn ho da no dodow so, efisɛ eyi ma asiane a ɛwɔ hɔ sɛ obi benya STI ahorow no so tew. Wo ne Ahokafo nni Nkitaho: Wo ne wɔn a wɔne wɔn da no mmɔ nkɔmmɔ a emu da hɔ na fa nokwaredi fa STI, sɔhwɛ, ne nyinsɛn a wosiw ano ho. Yɛ Sɔhwɛ: Yɛ nhwehwɛmu daa sɛ wowɔ STIs, titiriw sɛ wowɔ ɔhokafo foforo anaa ahokafo pii a. Susuw Awo ano aduro ho: Fa awo ano aduro a wotumi de ho to so, te sɛ akwan a wɔfa so de hormone anaa nnuru a wɔde siw nyinsɛn ano a ɛyɛ adwuma bere tenten (LARC) di dwuma na asiw nyinsɛn a woanhyɛ da ano.

TargetRLF1: Gyinaesi nhyehyɛe ahorow a mmabun taa de di dwuma no bi ne: Gyinaesi nhyehyɛe a ntease wom: Saa nhwɛso yi si nsɛm a ɛfata a wɔbɛboaboa ano, akari mfaso ne ɔhaw ahorow a ɛwɔ ɔkwan biara so, na wɔapaw ɔkwan foforo a ɛma mfaso kɛse ne asiane ahorow so tew so dua. Egyina nsusuwii a ntease wom ne nhwehwɛmu a wɔhyɛ da yɛ so. Suban ho gyinaesi nhwɛso: Saa nhwɛso yi gye nkɛntɛnso a nkate, awosu, ne asetra mu nneɛma nya wɔ gyinaesi so tom. Egye tom sɛ ebia ɛnyɛ bere nyinaa na ankorankoro paw nneɛma a ntease wom katee na ɛde nkate ne asetra mu gyinapɛn ahorow bi ka gyinaesi nhyehyɛe no ho. Asetra mu gyinaesi nhyehyɛe: Saa nhwɛso yi twe adwene si sɛnea asetra mu nkɛntɛnso te sɛ atipɛnfo nhyɛso, abusua mu nkɔso, ne amammerɛ mu gyinapɛn ahorow hyehyɛ gyinaesi so. Ɛsi nsunsuanso a obi paw nya wɔ afoforo so ne asetra mu tebea a ɛtrɛw no a wobesusuw so dua. Wobetumi de saa nhwɛso ahorow yi adi dwuma wɔ asetra mu tebea horow ankasa a ɛfa atipɛnfo nhyɛso ne nna mu nneyɛe ho denam mmabun a wɔbɛkyerɛ wɔn kwan wɔ nhyehyɛe bi a wɔde bɛsɔ nneɛma a wobetumi apaw mu, asusuw nea ebetumi afi mu aba ho, ne nneɛma a wɔpaw a wɔde wɔn yiyedi ne wɔn gyinapɛn ahorow di kan no so.

TargetRLF1: Ɛnyɛ akwan a wɔfa so siw awo ano a wɔde honam mu nkwammoa di dwuma nyinaa na ɛhwehwɛ sɛ wogye wɔn ahome. Sukuu mu Ayaresabea ahorow: Saa ayaresabea yi ma nkyerɛkyerɛ a ɛfa kwan a wɔfa so si STI ne HIV kwan, sɔhwɛ, ne afutuo dwumadie wɔ sukuu mu, na ama asuafoɔ atumi anya akwahosan ho mmoa ntɛm. Mpɔtam Akwahosan Adwumayɛfo Dwumadie: Akwahosan adwumayɛfoɔ a wɔatete wɔn kɔ afie-afie kɔma nkyerɛkyerɛ, sɔhwɛ, ne afutuo dwumadie; eyi ma nkurɔfoɔ a wɔte mmeaeɛ a ɛhɔ yɛ sum anaa mmeaeɛ a mmoa nni hɔ no nsa ka nkyerɛkyerɛ. Abɛɛfo ntentan so dwumadie ma kwan ma dɔkotanom ne nkurɔfoɔ di nkɔmmɔ kɔ akyiri, ɛma nkurɔfoɔ nsa ka sɔhwɛ-nnaka a wɔde sɔ wɔn ho hwɛ, ne nkyerɛkyerɛ mmoa; eyi trɛw STI ne HIV ahobammɔ nkyerɛkyerɛ mu, titiriw wɔ nkuraaseɛ mmeaeɛ a mmoa nni hɔ. TargetRLF1: Osuahu a Wɔbɛkyɛ: Asɛnka agua a wɔbɛma mmabun anya wɔn suahu, apereperedi, ne nkonimdi ahorow akyerɛ afoforo a wɔte ase. Gyetum ne moborhun: Wɔde gyetum, tema, ne mmoa a ɛnyɛ atemmuo a ɛfiri atipɛnfoɔ a wɔwɔ osuahu a ɛte saa ara hɔ bɛma. Nsɛm ne Nneɛma a Wɔde Yɛ: Nsɛm, nneɛma, akwan a wɔfa so gyina ano, ne afotu a wɔde hwɛ wɔn ho a ɛfa adwene mu apomden yareɛ ne yiyedi ho a wɔbɛkyɛ. Tew a Tew Wɔn Ho a Wɔtew So: Tew a wɔatew wɔn ho ho nkate a wɔbɛko atia denam mmabun a wɔde bɛka atipɛnfo kuw bi a wɔboa wɔn a wobetumi ne wɔn suahu ahorow adi nkitaho no so. Nkɔsoɔ a wɔbɛhyɛ ho nkuran: Anidasoɔ, ahoɔden a wɔde gyina ano, ne ahotɔ a wɔbɛhyɛ ho nkuran denam nkɔsoɔ ne nsɛnnennen a wɔdi so nkonim ho nsɛm a wɔkyɛ so.

TargetRLF1: Atemmu a Ɛnsɛe: Nsa ne nnuru a wode di dwuma betumi asɛe w’adwene ne wo gyinaesi. Ebetumi ama woanya nna mu nneyɛe a asiane wom anaa tebea horow a ebia wubenu wo ho. Nsɛm a Ɛfa Mpene Ho: Sɛ wonom nsa a, ebetumi ama wo tumi a wode bɛma anaasɛ wubenya pene a emu da hɔ no ayɛ mununkum. Ɛsɛ sɛ wɔpene so wɔ adwempa, anigye, na wɔde ma kwa. Asiane a Ɛbɛtew So: Kwati sɛ wode nsa anaa nnubɔne bɛfrafra nna mu dwumadi mu. Sɛ wopɛ sɛ wonom nsa a, fa asɛyɛde ne nea ɛfata yɛ saa. Nkitahodi: Wo ne wo hokafo nsusuw ahye ne nea wɔhwɛ kwan ho ansa na woanom nsa. Yɛ nhyehyɛe bi a ɛbɛma woanya ɔkwan a ahobammɔ wom a wobɛfa so akɔ fie sɛ woafi adi a. Hu Wo Anohyeto: Hu sɛnea nsa ka w’ankasa. Hu bere a wunni adwene tebea a ɛfata mu ma nna mu dwumadi.

TargetRLF1: If you test positive for an STI:  Seek Treatment: Contact your healthcare provider promptly to discuss treatment options and next steps. Follow Treatment Recommendations: Follow your provider's recommendations for treatment, which may include medications such as antibiotics, antivirals, or antiretrovirals, depending on the specific STI. Notify Sexual Partners: Inform recent sexual partners about your STI diagnosis so they can get tested and, if necessary, receive treatment to prevent further spread. Practice Safe Sex: Abstain from sexual activity or use condoms consistently and correctly to prevent the spread of infection to partners during treatment. Treatment options vary depending on the type of STI and individual factors, so it's essential to work closely with your healthcare provider to develop a personalized treatment plan.

TargetRLF1: Age of Consent: The age of consent is the minimum age at which a person is considered legally competent to consent to sexual activity. Laws vary by country and region, so it's important to know the specific laws where you live. In some places, the age of consent is 16, while in others, it may be 18 or even higher. Applicability: If you are below the age of consent, it is generally illegal to engage in sexual activity with someone older. Even if both parties consent, the law may still consider it illegal due to the age difference. Understanding Consent: Being at the age of consent doesn't mean you must engage in sexual activity. Consent should always be freely given, enthusiastic, and informed, regardless of age.

TargetRLF1: Some myths or misconceptions about reproductive health issues include:  Myth: "Birth control pills make you gain weight." Fact: While the likelihood is lower, it's still possible to get pregnant during menstruation, especially if the menstrual cycle is shorter or irregular. Myth: "You can't get an STI from oral sex." TargetRLF1: Respect and communication are key to healthy, safe sex. Here's why:  Informed Consent: Before any sexual activity, both partners should give informed consent. This means freely agreeing to what's happening without pressure or manipulation. You should both feel comfortable and understand the risks involved (like STIs and pregnancy). Respecting Boundaries: Everyone has sexual boundaries, which are the lines of what they feel comfortable with physically and emotionally. It's important to communicate your boundaries to your partner and respect theirs. This includes things like what types of touching are okay, what you're not comfortable with, and using protection (condoms). Remember: You always have the right to say no to any sexual activity, at any time. Don't feel pressured to do anything you're not comfortable with.

Check the expiration dates of your contraception methods regularly and replace them as needed to ensure effectiveness.

*   **AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome):** AIDS is the advanced stage of HIV infection. It is diagnosed when a person with HIV has a severely compromised immune system, as indicated by a very low CD4+ T cell count or the presence of certain opportunistic infections or cancers. People with AIDS are more susceptible to life-threatening illnesses and conditions because their immune system is severely damaged.

TargetRLF1: Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP) is typically used before potential exposure to HIV, not after. PrEP is a preventive strategy in which individuals at high risk of HIV infection take a medication (usually a daily pill) to reduce their risk of contracting the virus if they are exposed to it. It's important to note that PrEP is not intended to be used as a post-exposure treatment (after potential exposure to HIV). In cases where there has been a potential exposure to HIV, a different strategy known as Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) may be considered. PEP involves taking a 28-day course of antiretroviral medications after a potential exposure to HIV to reduce the risk of infection. If you believe you may be at risk of HIV infection and are considering PrEP, it is essential to consult with a healthcare provider who can assess your specific circumstances, provide guidance on PrEP use, and ensure that it is the appropriate preventive strategy for you.

TargetRLF1: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) is primarily transmitted through certain body fluids, including blood, semen, vaginal fluids, rectal fluids, and breast milk. Transmission can occur through sexual contact, sharing needles for drug use, or from mother to child during childbirth or breastfeeding. However, the risk of transmission can be greatly reduced through various prevention methods and by managing the virus effectively. If someone is living with HIV and has a low viral load (the amount of HIV in their blood is undetectable or very low) due to effective antiretroviral therapy (ART), the risk of transmitting the virus to a sexual partner is significantly reduced. This is often referred to as "Undetectable = Untransmittable" or "U=U." When the virus is undetectable, the chances of transmitting HIV to a negative partner are considered to be extremely low, although not zero. However, it's essential to note that while the risk is reduced, it is not completely eliminated. The use of other prevention methods, such as consistent and correct condom use, pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for the negative partner, and regular medical check-ups, can further reduce the risk of transmission.It's crucial for individuals living with HIV and their partners to communicate openly about their HIV status, engage in safe sexual practices, and work with healthcare professionals to manage the virus effectively. Additionally, regular testing and adherence to medical advice are vital aspects of HIV prevention and care. It's always advisable to seek guidance from healthcare professionals for personalized information and recommendations based on individual circumstances.

The risk of HIV infection is related to behaviors and practices that expose individuals to the virus, rather than their blood type.

TargetRLF1: Menstrual Disorders: Issues like irregular periods, heavy bleeding (menorrhagia), painful periods (dysmenorrhea), and absent periods (amenorrhea) can occur. Treatments vary depending on the issue and may include medications, hormonal therapy, lifestyle changes, or surgery. Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS): PCOS is a hormonal disorder that affects females' reproductive systems. It can cause irregular periods, excess hair growth, acne, and ovarian cysts. Management includes lifestyle changes (diet and exercise), medications to regulate hormones, and managing symptoms. Endometriosis: This is a condition where tissue similar to the lining of the uterus grows outside the uterus, causing pain, heavy periods, and infertility. Treatment options include medication, surgery to remove the tissue, and hormone therapy. Erectile Dysfunction: In males, this is the inability to achieve or maintain an erection. Causes can include physical issues, psychological factors, or underlying health conditions. Treatments may include medications, lifestyle changes, therapy, or surgery. Infertility: Difficulty getting pregnant can be due to various factors in both males and females. Treatment options depend on the underlying cause and may include medications, surgery, assisted reproductive technologies (such as IVF), or lifestyle changes. STIs: As mentioned earlier, prevention is key through sexual abstinence, remaining mutually faithful to a sexual partner, being in a monogamous sexual relationship for a long time, correct and consistent use of condoms, regular testing, and communication with sexual partners. Vaccinations against HPV and Hepatitis B are very important. If an STI is contracted, treatment is available, usually in the form of antibiotics (for bacterial infections) or antiviral medications (for viral infections). It's important to seek medical advice if you experience any reproductive health issues or concerns. Early detection and treatment can often lead to better outcomes. Always remember to complete taking the prescribed medication and go for review.

TargetRLF1: The best way to protect yourself from HIV is to abstain from having sex. However, if you cannot abstain, be faithful to your partner and use condoms. If you engage in high-risk sexual behaviors, then consult your health care provider about pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) medicine taken once a person anticipates being exposed to HIV. Also, there is post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP), a form of ARV that one takes to prevent acquiring HIV after exposure to a risky situation that leads to HIV infection, such as unprotected sexual intercourse or exposure to body fluids of an HIV-infected person. Prevention of transmission of HIV from an infected mother to her unborn child is also possible by the mother adhering to her ARVs well during pregnancy, attending antenatal care well, and delivering under the care of a qualified birth attendant who implements the safe delivery practices to protect the baby from acquiring HIV from its mother. Once the baby is born, it is given a preventive ARV syrup and followed up until 18 months of life to ensure it never acquires the virus. Safe male medical circumcision reduces the risk of contracting HIV. There are advanced research studies concerning the HIV vaccine. This is aimed at preventing the spread of HIV.

TargetRLF1: Chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary Infection often has no symptoms, but it can cause serious health problems, even without symptoms. If symptoms occur, they may not appear until several weeks after having sex with a partner who has chlamydia. Even when chlamydia has no symptoms, it can damage a woman's reproductive system. Women with symptoms of Genital infection may notice: An abnormal vaginal discharge, A burning sensation when peeing. Men with symptoms of Genital infection may notice: A discharge from their penis, A burning sensation when peeing, Pain and swelling in one or both testicles (although this is less common). Symptoms from rectal infections. Men and women can also get chlamydia in their rectum. This happens either by having receptive anal sex, or by spread from another infected site (such as the vagina). While these infections often cause no symptoms, they can cause: Rectal pain, Discharge, Bleeding. If a patient has Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV), initial symptoms may include genital ulcers, followed by painful swelling of lymph nodes in the groin. If untreated, it can lead to severe complications such as rectal strictures.  Throat infections can cause symptoms such as swelling in the back of the throat and mild sore throat, but symptoms are rare.

Herpes is a lifelong sexually transmitted infection, that cannot be cured. However, the number of outbreaks/recurrences may reduce over time.

Common symptoms of genital infection in men include: burning when urinating, discharge from the penis (mucus-like or clear watery discharge), pain or discomfort in the testicles. Anal infection in women and men can cause: pain, mucus-like discharge and bleeding. If untreated, it can lead to severe complications such as rectal strictures.

TargetRLF1: Trichomoniasis or trich is tested using several methods, which can involve laboratory tests to detect the presence of Trichomonas vaginalis. Here are the common testing methods:1.	Microscopic Examination:o	Wet Mount: A sample of vaginal fluid (from women) or urethral fluid (from men) is placed on a glass slide and examined under a microscope. The presence of motile Trichomonas vaginalis organisms can confirm the infection. This method requires immediate examination as the organisms can lose motility quickly.2.	Culture Test:o	A sample is placed in a culture medium that promotes the growth of Trichomonas vaginalis. This method is more sensitive than a wet mount and can detect the infection even when the parasite count is low. Results typically take several days.3.	Nucleic Acid Amplification Tests (NAATs):o	These tests are highly sensitive and specific, detecting the genetic material of Trichomonas vaginalis. NAATs can be performed on various sample types, including vaginal swabs, urine samples (both men and women), and urethral swabs. Results are generally available within a day or two.4.	Rapid Antigen Tests:o	These tests detect the presence of Trichomonas vaginalis antigens in a sample. They provide results quickly, often within minutes, and are useful for point-of-care testing.5.	Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR):o	Similar to NAATs, PCR tests amplify the DNA of Trichomonas vaginalis to detect its presence. This method is highly accurate and can be performed on various types of samples.Sample Collection Methods:•	Vaginal Swab: Commonly used for women, a swab is taken from the vaginal walls or cervix.•	Urethral Swab: Used for men, a swab is taken from the urethra.

Common oral herpes symptoms include blisters (cold sores) or open sores (ulcers) in or around the mouth or lips. Most people notice a group or cluster of blisters or ulcers (lesions). These lesions burn and can be painful. They can appear on the buttocks, anus or thighs, on the vulva or vagina in women, and on the penis or scrotum in men. Often, before the lesions appear, patients describe a prodrome, characterized by a tingling or burning sensation in the area where the lesions will develop that can be noticed during urination, along with itching or discomfort in the genital area. You can also have the following symptoms, Blisters on the mouth or lips, Fever, headache or pain in the joints, Trouble urinating. In case of Neonatal Herpes, signs that your baby may have herpes are:irritability, blisters anywhere on her body, trouble breathing, grunting, blue appearance (cyanosis), rapid breathing, short periods of no breathing, jaundice, bleeding easily. People can have repeated outbreaks over time (‘recurrences’). These are usually shorter and less severe than the first outbreak. Herpes is a lifelong sexually transmitted infection, that cannot be cured. However, the number of outbreaks/recurrences may reduce over time.

Culture performed on an intraurethral swab specimen is preferred in settings where collecting such specimens is acceptable and transport and storage conditions are suitable for culture. Collection of exudate for N. gonorrhoeae culture at the urethral meatus without insertion of the swab into the urethra is sufficient when exudate is present. Culture performed on an intraurethral swab specimen is preferred because it facilitates additional testing, which is critical for monitoring for antimicrobial resistance. When culture sensitivity might be compromised because of problems in maintaining appropriate transport and storage conditions, a NAAT or nucleic acid hybridization test can be performed on an intraurethral swab specimen.2.        A NAAT or nucleic acid hybridization test performed on an intraurethral swab specimen or a NAAT performed on urine are acceptable tests for screening. Intraurethral swab specimens are often unacceptable to males who do not have symptoms of urethritis (89--91). In such cases, NAATs performed on urine to detect N. gonorrhoeae have adequate sensitivity for use in screening. Only a limited number of published studies have addressed whether NAATs perform better with urethral swab specimens than with urine in males with asymptomatic infection. One study of asymptomatic males reported that a NAAT was substantially less sensitive with urine than with urethral swabs; furthermore, NAATs were less sensitive with either specimen among males with asymptomatic infection than with symptomatic infection (54,56).

Hippocrates (460–375 BC) called it "Strangury." The disease was also referred to as “The Clap,” referring either to the “clapping” feeling experienced by the infected person during urination. In those days, men were considered victims and women as the source of the disease. The organism that causes the disease was first discovered in 1879 in Albert Neisser, hence the organisma being called Neisseria Gonorrhoea. The word Gonorrhea itself derives from the Greek, meaning "the flow of seed" (gono: seed, rhea: flow).

TargetRLF1: A chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary infection test detects the bacteria that cause chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI). This test tells you if you have the chlamydia infection so that you can get treat treatment and avoid complications.. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs your vagina, urethra or anus to collect a sample of fluid and cells. They send the cells to a lab to test. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Your need for testing depends on your personal risk for getting Chlamydia. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear and you may need to give another sample. You should get tested regularly if you have unprotected sex with a new partner, multiple partners or a partner who has an STI. You should also get tested if you have symptoms or you are pregnant. Many people with Chlamydia don't have symptoms. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for chlamydial infection.

TargetRLF1: Syphilis is treatable and curable. People who suspect they may have syphilis should speak to their healthcare provider. The early stage of syphilis is treated with a benzathine penicillin (BPG) injection. BPG is the first line treatment for syphilis and the only WHO-recommended treatment for pregnant women with syphilis. As second line treatment, doctors may also use doxycycline, ceftriaxone or azithromycin, which are antibiotic medicines is also used to treat later stages of syphilis, but more doses are required. Doses are usually given once per week for three weeks, including when it is not possible to identify the stage of infection. Your partner should also get treatment. Do not have sex (vaginal, anal or oral) until you and your partner have finished treatment and tests have shown treatment has worked. BPG can prevent syphilis from being passed from a mother to baby. Babies born with syphilis (congenital syphilis), or babies whose mother had untreated syphilis, need to be treated right away to avoid serious health problems. Aqueous benzyl penicillin or procaine penicillin is recommended in infants with confirmed congenital syphilis or infants who are clinically healthy but whose mothers had untreated syphilis, inadequately treated syphilis (including treatment within 30 days of delivery), or syphilis treated with non-penicillin regimens. Unfortunately, while Syphilis can be cured, there is no way to repair organs damaged by syphilis.

TargetRLF1: A chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary infection test detects the bacteria that cause chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI). This test tells you if you have the chlamydia infection so that you can get treat treatment and avoid complications. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs your vagina, urethra or anus to collect a sample of fluid and cells. They send the cells to a lab to test. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for chlamydial infection. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear and you may need to give another sample.

TargetRLF1: While some people with Genital Herpes or Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV), also known as herpes will never have any symptoms, other people can develop symptoms within a few weeks of being infected. Common oral herpes symptoms include blisters (cold sores) or open sores (ulcers) in or around the mouth or lips. Most people notice a group or cluster of blisters or ulcers (lesions). These lesions burn and can be painful. They can appear on the buttocks, anus or thighs, on the vulva or vagina in women, and on the penis or scrotum in men. Often, before the lesions appear, patients describe a prodrome, characterized by a tingling or burning sensation in the area where the lesions will develop that can be noticed during urination, along with itching or discomfort in the genital area. You can also have the following symptoms, Blisters on the mouth or lips, Fever, headache or pain in the joints, Trouble urinating. In case of Neonatal Herpes, signs that your baby may have herpes are:irritability, blisters anywhere on her body, trouble breathing, grunting, blue appearance (cyanosis), rapid breathing, short periods of no breathing, jaundice, bleeding easily. People can have repeated outbreaks over time (‘recurrences’). These are usually shorter and less severe than the first outbreak. Herpes is a lifelong sexually transmitted infection, that cannot be cured. However, the number of outbreaks/recurrences may reduce over time.

TargetRLF1: Medicines are often used to treat first or recurrent episodes of Genital herpes or Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV), also known as Herpes. They can decrease how long symptoms last and how severe they are, but they can’t cure the infection. Treatment for recurrent episodes is most effective when started within 48 hours of when symptoms begin. Antiviral medicines commonly given include acyclovir, famciclovir and valacyclovir. Taking a lower daily dose of one of these medicines can also decrease how often symptoms occur (‘outbreaks’). Treatment is often recommended for people who get very painful or frequent recurrent episodes or who want to lower the risk of giving herpes to someone else. Medicines to help with pain related to sores include paracetamol (acetaminophen), naproxen or ibuprofen. Medicines that can be applied to numb the affected area include benzocaine and lidocaine.  Herpes simplex virus lives inside of nerve cells and alternates between being inactive and active. Certain triggers can make the virus active including:•        illness or fever•        sun exposure•        menstrual period•        injury•        emotional stress•        surgery.For people whose oral herpes is activated by sunlight, avoiding sun exposure and wearing sunscreen can lower the risk of recurrences. To decrease symptoms of oral herpes, people can: •        drink cold drinks or suck on popsicles•        use over-the-counter pain medicines.For genital herpes, people can:•        sit in a warm bath for 20 minutes (without soap)•        wear loose fitting clothes•        use over-the-counter pain medicines.There are ways to lower the risk of spreading herpes including:•        talk to your partner about having herpes•        don’t have sex if you have symptoms and always wear a condom•        don’t share items that touched saliva (oral herpes).Talk to your healthcare provider if you are pregnant, because there is a risk of passing herpes to your baby.

TargetRLF1: PrEP (Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis) is not a myth; it is a real and effective HIV prevention strategy. It's important to note that PrEP does not protect against other sexually transmitted infections (STIs) or bloodborne infections, so individuals should continue to use condoms and practice safe sex to reduce the risk of other infections. PrEP is a valuable tool in the fight against HIV, especially for individuals who are at high risk. If you believe you are at risk of HIV infection or have questions about PrEP, it's advisable to consult with a healthcare provider who can assess your risk factors and provide guidance on whether PrEP is a suitable option for you.

Congenital syphilis can only be prevented by diagnosing and treating the mother.

Medicines can reduce symptoms but can’t cure the infection.Recurrent symptoms of both oral and genital herpes may be distressing. Genital herpes may also be stigmatizing and have an impact on sexual relationships. However, in time, most people with either kind of herpes adjust to living with the infection.

Secondary syphilis: symptoms include skin rash, wounds in the private parts, fever, sore throat, swollen lymph nodes, headaches, weight loss, muscle pains, fatigue, and many more. TargetRLF1: Human immunodeficiency virus is a zoonosis – an infection that originated in animals and passed into human hosts. SIV probably entered the human population as a result of hunting chimpanzees. It is plausible that transmission could have occurred through a hunter being wounded and blood from a chimpanzee entering his bloodstream. Chimpanzees avoid humans wherever possible, so it is likely that SIVcpz crossed into humans as a result of human encroachment into forest areas. The origin of the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) has been a subject of scientific research and debate since the virus was identified in the 1980s. It came to global attention after doctors started receiving reports of rare cases of pneumonia, cancer and other illnesses in young gay homosexual men and injection drug users. Over the decades, the disease has spread all over the world.

TargetRLF1: A chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary infection test detects the bacteria that cause chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI). This test tells you if you have the chlamydia infection so that you can get treat treatment and avoid complications.. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs your vagina, urethra or anus to collect a sample of fluid and cells. They send the cells to a lab to test. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Your need for testing depends on your personal risk for getting Chlamydia. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear and you may need to give another sample. You should get tested regularly if you have unprotected sex with a new partner, multiple partners or a partner who has an STI. You should also get tested if you have symptoms or you are pregnant. Many people with Chlamydia don't have symptoms. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for chlamydial infection.

TargetRLF1: Chlamydia infections also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary Infections spread through sexual contact, when vaginal fluid or semen containing the bacteria that causes chlamydia travels from one person to another. Sexual contact includes all kinds of sex, including sex that doesn’t involve penetration or ejaculation. There are lots of ways that the fluids from one person’s genitals can transmit the bacteria that causes chlamydia. Intercourse; bacteria pass from one person’s penis to their partner’s vagina or vice versa. Anal sex; bacteria pass from one person’s penis to their partner’s anus or vice versa. Sex involving toys; bacteria pass from a toy to a person’s mouth, penis, vagina or anus or through sharing sex toys that are not washed or covered with a new condom each time they are used. Oral sex; bacteria pass from one person’s mouth to their partner’s penis, vagina or anus, or vice versa. Manual stimulation of the genitals or anus; less commonly, infected vaginal fluid or semen can come in contact with a person’s eye, causing an infection called conjunctivitis (pink eye). For example, this can happen if you touch the genitals of an infected person and then rub your eyes without washing your hands first. A pregnant person with chlamydia can give the infection to their baby during childbirth and that can cause a type of eye infection in the baby which can damage their sight.

TargetRLF1: Taking care of your hair and scalp is important during puberty. Here are some tips: Shampoo regularly: Wash your hair regularly with appropriate shampoo or soap, depending on your hair type. Apply conditioner to the ends of your hair, avoiding the scalp if it tends to be oily. Scalp massage: Massaging your scalp gently while shampooing can help improve circulation and promote healthy hair growth. Avoid hot water: Use lukewarm water to wash your hair, as hot water can strip the scalp of natural oils. Protect from Heat: Limit the use of heat styling tools such as flat irons and blow dryers, as they can damage hair. Combing/brushing: Use an appropriate comb and brush based on your hair type. The herpes virus is spread throughout your child’s body and can affect multiple organs, including the liver, brain, lungs, and kidney.A baby infected with herpes may not have all the symptoms of the disease.Most symptoms surface by the end of the baby's first week, while more severe central nervous system problems will not appear until the baby's second week.If left untreated, encephalitis and disseminated herpes infections are potentially fatal.People can have repeated outbreaks over time (‘recurrences’). However, the number of outbreaks/recurrences may reduce over time.

If you are sexually active, you can do the following things to lower your chances of getting Genital Herpes or Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV), also known as herpes:•        Being in a long-term mutually monogamous relationship with a partner who does not have herpes.•        Using condoms the right way every time you have sex.Be aware that not all herpes sores occur in areas that a condom can cover. Also tell them about any possible exposure to genital herpes. If you have genital herpes, you may need to take anti-herpes medicine towards the end of your pregnancy. TargetRLF1: HSV-1 is mainly transmitted via contact with the virus in sores, saliva or surfaces in or around the mouth. TargetRLF1: A Chlamydia test detects the bacteria that cause Chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI), so get treatment. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis, or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for Chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs/wipes your vagina, urethra, or anus to collect a sample and send it to a lab for testing. If bacteria are present, it indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of Chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for Chlamydial infection. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely, the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear, and you may need to give another sample.

TargetRLF1: Syphilis develops in stages. The symptoms vary with each stage. But the stages may overlap. And the symptoms don't always happen in the same order. You may be infected with syphilis bacteria without noticing any symptoms for years. Primary syphilis: The first symptom of syphilis is a small sore called a chancre (SHANG-kur) that appears 2-12 weeks after exposure to someone with Syphilis. The sore is often painless. It appears at the spot where the bacteria entered your body. Most people with syphilis develop only one chancre. Some people get more than one. The chancre often forms about three weeks after you come in contact with syphilis bacteria. Many people who have syphilis don't notice the chancre. That's because it's usually painless. It also may be hidden within the vagina or rectum. The chancre heals on its own within 3 to 6 weeks. Secondary syphilis: While the chancre heals or a few weeks after it heals a Syphilis rash may develop; often unitchy, may appear rough, red, or reddish-brown and may be faint. It usually starts on the body's trunk, including chest, stomach, pelvis, back, and limbs. In time, it also could appear on the limbs, the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet. Besides the rash, other symptoms may include wartlike sores, hair loss, muscle aches, fever, sore throat, and fatigue. Symptoms of secondary syphilis may go away on their own. But without treatment, they could come and go for months or years. Latent syphilis: If you aren't treated for syphilis, the disease moves from the secondary stage to the latent stage. This also is called the hidden stage because you have no symptoms. The latent stage can last for years. Your symptoms may never come back. But without treatment, the disease might lead to major health problems, also called complications. Tertiary syphilis: After the latent stage, up to 30% to 40% of people with syphilis who don't get treatment have complications known as tertiary syphilis. Here, they may develop permanently damaged heart, brain, muscles, bones, and eyes. Congenital syphilis: Pregnant people who have syphilis can pass the disease to their babies. Unborn babies can become infected through the organ that provides nutrients and oxygen in the womb, called the placenta. Infection also can happen during birth. Newborns with congenital syphilis might have no symptoms. But without fast treatment, some babies might get sores and rashes on the skin, fever, a type of discolored skin and eyes called jaundice, not enough red blood cells (anemia), swollen spleen and liver, sneezing or a stuffed, drippy nose called rhinitis, bone changes, weight loss, and swollen lymph nodes. Later symptoms may include deafness, teeth problems, and saddle nose, a condition in which the bridge of the nose collapses. Babies with syphilis also can be born too early. They may die in the womb before birth, or they could die after birth.

TargetRLF1: While some people with Genital Herpes or Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV), also known as herpes will never have any symptoms, other people can develop symptoms within a few weeks of being infected. Common oral herpes symptoms include blisters (cold sores) or open sores (ulcers) in or around the mouth or lips. Most people notice a group or cluster of blisters or ulcers (lesions). These lesions burn and can be painful. They can appear on the buttocks, anus or thighs, on the vulva or vagina in women, and on the penis or scrotum in men. Often, before the lesions appear, patients describe a prodrome, characterized by a tingling or burning sensation in the area where the lesions will develop that can be noticed during urination, along with itching or discomfort in the genital area. You can also have the following symptoms, Blisters on the mouth or lips, Fever, headache or pain in the joints, Trouble urinating. In case of Neonatal Herpes, signs that your baby may have herpes are:irritability, blisters anywhere on her body, trouble breathing, grunting, blue appearance (cyanosis), rapid breathing, short periods of no breathing, jaundice, bleeding easily. People can have repeated outbreaks over time (‘recurrences’). These are usually shorter and less severe than the first outbreak. Herpes is a lifelong sexually transmitted infection, that cannot be cured. However, the number of outbreaks/recurrences may reduce over time.

TargetRLF1: A chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary infection test detects the bacteria that cause chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI). This test tells you if you have the chlamydia infection so that you can get treat treatment and avoid complications.. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs your vagina, urethra or anus to collect a sample of fluid and cells. They send the cells to a lab to test. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Your need for testing depends on your personal risk for getting Chlamydia. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear and you may need to give another sample. You should get tested regularly if you have unprotected sex with a new partner, multiple partners or a partner who has an STI. You should also get tested if you have symptoms or you are pregnant. Many people with Chlamydia don't have symptoms. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for chlamydial infection.

TargetRLF1: Gonorrhea is classified according to site or according to its responsiveness to treatment. According to site, there is urogenital, extragenital and disseminated gonorrhea. Urogenital Gonorrhea primarily affects the genital and urinary tract. Extragenital Gonorrhea occurs outside the genital area. It can infect the throat (from oral sex) or the rectum (from anal sex) or can manifest as an eye infection (gonoccocal conjuctivitis) in new born babies. Disseminated Gonococcal Infection is a more severe form where the infection spreads throughout the body. According to responsiveness to treatment, there is regular or multidrug resistant/super gonorrhea. Regular gonorrhea responds to commonly used antimicrobial drugs. However, multidrug resistant (super) gonorrhea is resistant to so many treatment options, including penicillins, sulphonamides, tetracyclines, quinolones and macrolides (including azithromycin), as well as so-called last line options like cephalosporins.

TargetRLF1: Human immunodeficiency virus is a zoonosis – an infection that originated in animals and passed into human hosts. It is closely related to the simian immunodeficiency virus (SIV) found in chimpanzees in central Africa. To date, the earliest known case of HIV-1 infection in human blood is from a sample taken in 1959 from a man who'd died in Kinshasa in what was then the Belgian Congo. SIV probably entered the human population as a result of hunting chimpanzees. It is plausible that transmission could have occurred through a hunter being wounded and blood from a chimpanzee entering his bloodstream. Chimpanzees avoid humans wherever possible, so it is likely that SIVcpz crossed into humans as a result of human encroachment into forest areas. The origin of the Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) has been a subject of scientific research and debate since the virus was identified in the 1980s. It came to global attention after doctors started receiving reports of rare cases of pneumonia, cancer and other illnesses in young gay homosexual men and injection drug users. Over the decades, the disease has spread all over the world.

TargetRLF1: Chlamydia infections also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary Infections spread through sexual contact, when vaginal fluid or semen containing the bacteria that causes chlamydia travels from one person to another. Sexual contact includes all kinds of sex, including sex that doesn’t involve penetration or ejaculation. There are lots of ways that the fluids from one person’s genitals can transmit the bacteria that causes chlamydia. Intercourse; bacteria pass from one person’s penis to their partner’s vagina or vice versa. Anal sex; bacteria pass from one person’s penis to their partner’s anus or vice versa. Sex involving toys; bacteria pass from a toy to a person’s mouth, penis, vagina or anus or through sharing sex toys that are not washed or covered with a new condom each time they are used. Oral sex; bacteria pass from one person’s mouth to their partner’s penis, vagina or anus, or vice versa. Manual stimulation of the genitals or anus; less commonly, infected vaginal fluid or semen can come in contact with a person’s eye, causing an infection called conjunctivitis (pink eye). For example, this can happen if you touch the genitals of an infected person and then rub your eyes without washing your hands first. A pregnant person with chlamydia can give the infection to their baby during childbirth and that can cause a type of eye infection in the baby which can damage their sight.

TargetRLF1: Syphilis is treatable and curable. People who suspect they may have syphilis should speak to their healthcare provider. The early stage of syphilis is treated with a benzathine penicillin (BPG) injection. BPG is the first line treatment for syphilis and the only WHO-recommended treatment for pregnant women with syphilis. As second line treatment, doctors may also use doxycycline, ceftriaxone or azithromycin, which are antibiotic medicines is also used to treat later stages of syphilis, but more doses are required. Doses are usually given once per week for three weeks, including when it is not possible to identify the stage of infection. Your partner should also get treatment. Do not have sex (vaginal, anal or oral) until you and your partner have finished treatment and tests have shown treatment has worked. BPG can prevent syphilis from being passed from a mother to baby. Babies born with syphilis (congenital syphilis), or babies whose mother had untreated syphilis, need to be treated right away to avoid serious health problems. Aqueous benzyl penicillin or procaine penicillin is recommended in infants with confirmed congenital syphilis or infants who are clinically healthy but whose mothers had untreated syphilis, inadequately treated syphilis (including treatment within 30 days of delivery), or syphilis treated with non-penicillin regimens. Unfortunately, while Syphilis can be cured, there is no way to repair organs damaged by syphilis.

TargetRLF1: Herpes can only be diagnosed at a health centre. You cannot test for it at home or using radiological methods. When a person has active herpes lesions (a.k.a. blisters, sores, ulcers), your healthcare provider may diagnose genital herpes by simply looking at any sores that are present, or a swab can be taken and tests done that can detect the virus directly. The tests used include culture and Nucleic Acid Amplification Testing (NAAT). Both culture and NAAT tests can identify the specific herpes virus type someone is infected with, but NAAT methods are more sensitive. Culture can easily miss infections and give false negative results. Because a culture works by requiring virus that is active, if a lesion is very small, or is already beginning to heal, there may not be enough virus present for an accurate culture. Beyond 48 hours of the symptoms appearing, there is a risk of receiving a false negative test result. Viral culture is even less accurate during recurrences (positive in only about 30% of recurrent outbreaks). NAAT methods are the preferred method, with PCR as the most-widely used NAAT method. These tests are fast, accurate, and can tell if a person has HSV-1 or HSV-2. There is also less chance of a false negative result with NAATs. If sores are not present, a blood test may be used to look for HSV antibodies. Blood tests can be used when a person has no symptoms but has concerns about having Herpes. Blood tests do not actually detect the virus but look for antibodies.

The disease, whose origin is still widely contested amongst researchers, arrived in Africa no later than the 16th century. It was first confirmed in Africa by 1520.

Fulminant hepatitis

TargetRLF1: It is unclear what the origins of the organism Chlamydia trachomatis are. The organism causes both Trachoma, an eye infection and Chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary Infection, a Sexually Transmitted Infection. Chlamydia trachomatis was first described in historical records in Ebers papyrus written between 1553 and 1550 BC. In the ancient world, it was known as the blinding disease, Trachoma. It seems likely that the organism evolved with humans and shared a common ancestor with environmental Chlamydiae some 700 million years ago. The organism is complex and was initially described as a protozoan in 1907 by Prowazeck and Halberstadter. It was later then thought to be a virus and it is only until 1966 when it was finally correctly understood to be a bacterium.  Chlamydia trachomatis causes acute eye infections, trachoma, genital infections and the more invasive sexually transmitted infection; lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV).

TargetRLF1: Syphilis develops in stages. The symptoms vary with each stage. But the stages may overlap. And the symptoms don't always happen in the same order. You may be infected with syphilis bacteria without noticing any symptoms for years. Primary syphilis: The first symptom of syphilis is a small sore called a chancre (SHANG-kur) that appears 2-12 weeks after exposure to someone with Syphilis. The sore is often painless. It appears at the spot where the bacteria entered your body. Most people with syphilis develop only one chancre. Some people get more than one. The chancre often forms about three weeks after you come in contact with syphilis bacteria. Many people who have syphilis don't notice the chancre. That's because it's usually painless. It also may be hidden within the vagina or rectum. The chancre heals on its own within 3 to 6 weeks. Secondary syphilis: While the chancre heals or a few weeks after it heals a Syphilis rash may develop; often unitchy, may appear rough, red, or reddish-brown and may be faint. It usually starts on the body's trunk, including chest, stomach, pelvis, back, and limbs. In time, it also could appear on the limbs, the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet. Besides the rash, other symptoms may include wartlike sores, hair loss, muscle aches, fever, sore throat, and fatigue. Symptoms of secondary syphilis may go away on their own. But without treatment, they could come and go for months or years. Latent syphilis: If you aren't treated for syphilis, the disease moves from the secondary stage to the latent stage. This also is called the hidden stage because you have no symptoms. The latent stage can last for years. Your symptoms may never come back. But without treatment, the disease might lead to major health problems, also called complications. Tertiary syphilis: After the latent stage, up to 30% to 40% of people with syphilis who don't get treatment have complications known as tertiary syphilis. Here, they may develop permanently damaged heart, brain, muscles, bones, and eyes. Congenital syphilis: Pregnant people who have syphilis can pass the disease to their babies. Unborn babies can become infected through the organ that provides nutrients and oxygen in the womb, called the placenta. Infection also can happen during birth. Newborns with congenital syphilis might have no symptoms. But without fast treatment, some babies might get sores and rashes on the skin, fever, a type of discolored skin and eyes called jaundice, not enough red blood cells (anemia), swollen spleen and liver, sneezing or a stuffed, drippy nose called rhinitis, bone changes, weight loss, and swollen lymph nodes. Later symptoms may include deafness, teeth problems, and saddle nose, a condition in which the bridge of the nose collapses. Babies with syphilis also can be born too early. They may die in the womb before birth, or they could die after birth.

TargetRLF1: A Chlamydia test detects the bacteria that cause Chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI), so get treatment. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis, or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for Chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs/wipes your vagina, urethra, or anus to collect a sample and send it to a lab for testing. If bacteria are present, it indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of Chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for Chlamydial infection. TargetRLF1: Chlamydia infections also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary Infections spread through sexual contact, when vaginal fluid or semen containing the bacteria that causes chlamydia travels from one person to another. Sexual contact includes all kinds of sex, including sex that doesn’t involve penetration or ejaculation. There are lots of ways that the fluids from one person’s genitals can transmit the bacteria that causes chlamydia. Intercourse; bacteria pass from one person’s penis to their partner’s vagina or vice versa. Anal sex; bacteria pass from one person’s penis to their partner’s anus or vice versa. Sex involving toys; bacteria pass from a toy to a person’s mouth, penis, vagina or anus or through sharing sex toys that are not washed or covered with a new condom each time they are used. Oral sex; bacteria pass from one person’s mouth to their partner’s penis, vagina or anus, or vice versa. Manual stimulation of the genitals or anus; less commonly, infected vaginal fluid or semen can come in contact with a person’s eye, causing an infection called conjunctivitis (pink eye). For example, this can happen if you touch the genitals of an infected person and then rub your eyes without washing your hands first. A pregnant person with chlamydia can give the infection to their baby during childbirth and that can cause a type of eye infection in the baby which can damage their sight.

TargetRLF1: Gonorrhea can be traced back to the earliest records of the human race. The Romans, Jews and Arabs all have documents referring to gonorrhea and each society had their own description of symptoms and treatment. The origins of Gonorrhea can be traced back to the Old Testament in the books of Leviticus and Genesis where it is described as a contagious, continuous passing out of semen and painful erection. The disease has been referred to by various names in different societies. The Roman physician Galen in 130 AD described the disease as an "involuntary escape of semen".   Hippocrates (460–375 BC) called it "Strangury." The disease was also referred to as “The Clap,” referring either to the “clapping” feeling experienced by the infected person during urination. In those days, men were considered victims and women as the source of the disease. The organism that causes the disease was first discovered in 1879 in Albert Neisser, hence the organisma being called Neisseria Gonorrhoea. The word Gonorrhea itself derives from the Greek, meaning "the flow of seed" (gono: seed, rhea: flow).

TargetRLF1: A Chlamydia test detects the bacteria that cause Chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI), so get treatment. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis, or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for Chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs/wipes your vagina, urethra, or anus to collect a sample and send it to a lab for testing. If bacteria are present, it indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of Chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for Chlamydial infection. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely, the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear, and you may need to give another sample.

Some research suggest that a genital herpes infection may lead to miscarriage or make it more likely to deliver your baby too early. You can pass herpes to your unborn child before birth, but it more commonly passes during delivery. This can lead to a deadly infection in your baby (called neonatal herpes). It is important that you avoid getting genital herpes during pregnancy. Tell your healthcare provider if you have ever had a genital herpes diagnosis or symptoms. Also tell them about any possible exposure to genital herpes. If you have genital herpes, you may need to take anti-herpes medicine towards the end of your pregnancy. This medicine may reduce your risk of having signs or symptoms of genital herpes when you deliver. At the time of delivery, your healthcare provider should carefully examine you for herpes sores. If you have signs or symptoms of genital herpes at delivery, a 'C-section' is likely to occur.

TargetRLF1: Syphilis develops in stages. The symptoms vary with each stage. But the stages may overlap. And the symptoms don't always happen in the same order. You may be infected with syphilis bacteria without noticing any symptoms for years. Primary syphilis: The first symptom of syphilis is a small sore called a chancre (SHANG-kur) that appears 2-12 weeks after exposure to someone with Syphilis. The sore is often painless. It appears at the spot where the bacteria entered your body. Most people with syphilis develop only one chancre. Some people get more than one. The chancre often forms about three weeks after you come in contact with syphilis bacteria. Many people who have syphilis don't notice the chancre. That's because it's usually painless. It also may be hidden within the vagina or rectum. The chancre heals on its own within 3 to 6 weeks. Secondary syphilis: While the chancre heals or a few weeks after it heals a Syphilis rash may develop; often unitchy, may appear rough, red, or reddish-brown and may be faint. It usually starts on the body's trunk, including chest, stomach, pelvis, back, and limbs. In time, it also could appear on the limbs, the palms of the hands and the soles of the feet. Besides the rash, other symptoms may include wartlike sores, hair loss, muscle aches, fever, sore throat, and fatigue. Symptoms of secondary syphilis may go away on their own. But without treatment, they could come and go for months or years. Latent syphilis: If you aren't treated for syphilis, the disease moves from the secondary stage to the latent stage. This also is called the hidden stage because you have no symptoms. The latent stage can last for years. Your symptoms may never come back. But without treatment, the disease might lead to major health problems, also called complications. Tertiary syphilis: After the latent stage, up to 30% to 40% of people with syphilis who don't get treatment have complications known as tertiary syphilis. Here, they may develop permanently damaged heart, brain, muscles, bones, and eyes. Congenital syphilis: Pregnant people who have syphilis can pass the disease to their babies. Unborn babies can become infected through the organ that provides nutrients and oxygen in the womb, called the placenta. Infection also can happen during birth. Newborns with congenital syphilis might have no symptoms. But without fast treatment, some babies might get sores and rashes on the skin, fever, a type of discolored skin and eyes called jaundice, not enough red blood cells (anemia), swollen spleen and liver, sneezing or a stuffed, drippy nose called rhinitis, bone changes, weight loss, and swollen lymph nodes. Later symptoms may include deafness, teeth problems, and saddle nose, a condition in which the bridge of the nose collapses. Babies with syphilis also can be born too early. They may die in the womb before birth, or they could die after birth.

TargetRLF1: Gonorrhea can be traced back to the earliest records of the human race. The Romans, Jews and Arabs all have documents referring to gonorrhea and each society had their own description of symptoms and treatment. The origins of Gonorrhea can be traced back to the Old Testament in the books of Leviticus and Genesis where it is described as a contagious, continuous passing out of semen and painful erection. The disease has been referred to by various names in different societies. The Roman physician Galen in 130 AD described the disease as an "involuntary escape of semen".   Hippocrates (460–375 BC) called it "Strangury." The disease was also referred to as “The Clap,” referring either to the “clapping” feeling experienced by the infected person during urination. In those days, men were considered victims and women as the source of the disease. The organism that causes the disease was first discovered in 1879 in Albert Neisser, hence the organisma being called Neisseria Gonorrhoea. The word Gonorrhea itself derives from the Greek, meaning "the flow of seed" (gono: seed, rhea: flow).

TargetRLF1: Herpes can only be diagnosed at a health centre. You cannot test for it at home or using radiological methods. When a person has active herpes lesions (a.k.a. blisters, sores, ulcers), your healthcare provider may diagnose genital herpes by simply looking at any sores that are present, or a swab can be taken and tests done that can detect the virus directly. The tests used include culture and Nucleic Acid Amplification Testing (NAAT). Both culture and NAAT tests can identify the specific herpes virus type someone is infected with, but NAAT methods are more sensitive. Culture can easily miss infections and give false negative results. Because a culture works by requiring virus that is active, if a lesion is very small, or is already beginning to heal, there may not be enough virus present for an accurate culture. Beyond 48 hours of the symptoms appearing, there is a risk of receiving a false negative test result. Viral culture is even less accurate during recurrences (positive in only about 30% of recurrent outbreaks). NAAT methods are the preferred method, with PCR as the most-widely used NAAT method. These tests are fast, accurate, and can tell if a person has HSV-1 or HSV-2. There is also less chance of a false negative result with NAATs. If sores are not present, a blood test may be used to look for HSV antibodies. Blood tests can be used when a person has no symptoms but has concerns about having Herpes. Blood tests do not actually detect the virus but look for antibodies.

TargetRLF1: Chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary Infection often has no symptoms, but it can cause serious health problems, even without symptoms. If symptoms occur, they may not appear until several weeks after having sex with a partner who has chlamydia. In women, common symptoms of genital infection include: a change in vaginal discharge (white, yellow or grey discharge that may be smelly) , bleeding between menstrual periods or after sex, pain or discomfort in the lower abdomen and burning sensation when urinating. Common symptoms of genital infection in men include: burning when urinating, discharge from the penis (mucus-like or clear watery discharge), pain or discomfort in the testicles. Anal infection in women and men can cause: pain, mucus-like discharge and bleeding. If a patient has Lymphogranuloma Venereum (LGV), initial symptoms may include genital ulcers, followed by painful swelling of lymph nodes in the groin. If untreated, it can lead to severe complications such as rectal strictures.

TargetRLF1: Syphilis is treatable and it heals. People who suspect they may have syphilis should speak to their healthcare provider. The early stage of syphilis is treated with a benzathine penicillin (BPG) injection. BPG is the first line treatment for syphilis and the only World Health Organization (WHO)-recommended treatment for pregnant women with syphilis. As second line treatment, doctors may also use doxycycline, ceftriaxone or azithromycin, which are antibiotic medicines is also used to treat later stages of syphilis, but more doses are required. Doses are usually given once per week for three weeks, including when it is not possible to identify the stage of infection. Your partner should also get treatment. Do not have sex (vaginal, anus or mouth) until you and your partner(s) have finished treatment and tests have shown treatment has worked. BPG can prevent syphilis from being passed from a mother to baby. Babies born with syphilis (congenital syphilis), or babies whose mother had untreated syphilis, need to be treated right away to avoid serious health problems. Aqueous benzyl penicillin or procaine penicillin is recommended in infants with confirmed syphilis or infants who are clinically healthy but whose mothers had untreated syphilis, inadequately treated syphilis (including treatment within 30 days of giving birth), or syphilis treated with non-penicillin medicines. Unfortunately, while Syphilis can heal, there is no way to repair organs damaged by syphilis.

TargetRLF1: A chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary infection test detects the bacteria that cause chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI). This test tells you if you have the chlamydia infection so that you can get treat treatment and avoid complications.. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs your vagina, urethra or anus to collect a sample of fluid and cells. They send the cells to a lab to test. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Your need for testing depends on your personal risk for getting Chlamydia. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear and you may need to give another sample. You should get tested regularly if you have unprotected sex with a new partner, multiple partners or a partner who has an STI. You should also get tested if you have symptoms or you are pregnant. Many people with Chlamydia don't have symptoms. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for chlamydial infection.

TargetRLF1: Medicines are often used to treat first or recurrent episodes of Genital herpes or Herpes Simplex Virus (HSV), also known as Herpes. They can decrease how long symptoms last and how severe they are, but they can’t cure the infection. Treatment for recurrent episodes is most effective when started within 48 hours of when symptoms begin. Antiviral medicines commonly given include acyclovir, famciclovir and valacyclovir. Taking a lower daily dose of one of these medicines can also decrease how often symptoms occur (‘outbreaks’). Treatment is often recommended for people who get very painful or frequent recurrent episodes or who want to lower the risk of giving herpes to someone else. Medicines to help with pain related to sores include paracetamol (acetaminophen), naproxen or ibuprofen. Medicines that can be applied to numb the affected area include benzocaine and lidocaine.  Herpes simplex virus lives inside of nerve cells and alternates between being inactive and active. Certain triggers can make the virus active including:•        illness or fever•        sun exposure•        menstrual period•        injury•        emotional stress•        surgery.For people whose oral herpes is activated by sunlight, avoiding sun exposure and wearing sunscreen can lower the risk of recurrences. To decrease symptoms of oral herpes, people can: •        drink cold drinks or suck on popsicles•        use over-the-counter pain medicines.For genital herpes, people can:•        sit in a warm bath for 20 minutes (without soap)•        wear loose fitting clothes•        use over-the-counter pain medicines.There are ways to lower the risk of spreading herpes including:•        talk to your partner about having herpes•        don’t have sex if you have symptoms and always wear a condom•        don’t share items that touched saliva (oral herpes).Talk to your healthcare provider if you are pregnant, because there is a risk of passing herpes to your baby.

TargetRLF1: A chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary infection test detects the bacteria that cause chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI). This test tells you if you have the chlamydia infection so that you can get treat treatment and avoid complications. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs your vagina, urethra or anus to collect a sample of fluid and cells. They send the cells to a lab to test. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for chlamydial infection. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear and you may need to give another sample.

TargetRLF1: The signs and symptoms of an HIV infection can vary from person to person, and the timeline for when they appear can also differ. Here are some key points to consider: Acute HIV Infection: In the earliest stage of HIV infection, known as acute HIV infection or acute retroviral syndrome, some individuals may experience flu-like symptoms. These symptoms can occur within 2 to 4 weeks after exposure to the virus but can vary. Common symptoms during this stage may include fever, fatigue, sore throat, swollen lymph nodes, rash, muscle aches, and headache. However, not everyone with HIV will experience these symptoms, and they can be easily mistaken for other illnesses. Asymptomatic Stage: After the acute stage, many people with HIV enter a period called the clinical latency stage. During this phase, which can last for years, most individuals do not experience noticeable symptoms related to HIV. This is why HIV is sometimes referred to as a "silent" infection during this time. Advanced HIV (AIDS): Without treatment, HIV infection can progress to AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome). AIDS is characterized by a severely weakened immune system and the development of opportunistic infections or certain cancers. The timeline for progressing to AIDS can vary greatly from person to person and depends on multiple factors, including access to medical care and the use of antiretroviral therapy (ART).

The organism causes both Trachoma, an eye infection and Chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary Infection, a Sexually Transmitted Infection. TargetRLF1: A chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary infection test detects the bacteria that cause chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI). This test tells you if you have the chlamydia infection so that you can get treat treatment and avoid complications. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs your vagina, urethra or anus to collect a sample of fluid and cells. They send the cells to a lab to test. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for chlamydial infection. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear and you may need to give another sample.

TargetRLF1: Gonorrhea is classified according to site or according to its responsiveness to treatment. According to site, there is urogenital, extragenital and disseminated gonorrhea. Urogenital Gonorrhea primarily affects the genital and urinary tract. Extragenital Gonorrhea occurs outside the genital area. It can infect the throat (from oral sex) or the rectum (from anal sex) or can manifest as an eye infection (gonoccocal conjuctivitis) in new born babies. Disseminated Gonococcal Infection is a more severe form where the infection spreads throughout the body. According to responsiveness to treatment, there is regular or multidrug resistant/super gonorrhea. Regular gonorrhea responds to commonly used antimicrobial drugs. However, multidrug resistant (super) gonorrhea is resistant to so many treatment options, including penicillins, sulphonamides, tetracyclines, quinolones and macrolides (including azithromycin), as well as so-called last line options like cephalosporins.

TargetRLF1: Syphilis is a preventable disease. The only way to prevent Syphilis and other STIs is to abstain from sex. If sexually active, you can avoid transmission of syphilis by being in a long-term mutually monogamous relationship with a partner who has been tested and is known to be uninfected. However, since many primary lesions in the vagina & rectum go unnoticed, using condoms consistently and correctly prevents spread of syphilis. Syphilis can also spread through contact with other areas of the body not covered by a condom, including genitals, anus and mouth. Most people are unaware that they have been exposed to syphilis until notified by a partner or health provider. Partner notification is a service that can be provided by your nearest health facility. Sexual partners of infected patients are very likely to be infected. They should receive preventive treatment as per the treatment guidelines at the time of visit, without waiting for diagnostic results. Pregnant women should be tested for syphilis at the first prenatal care visit and treated right away if the test result is positive. Congenital syphilis can only be prevented by diagnosing and treating the mother.

TargetRLF1: Gonorrhea can be traced back to the earliest records of the human race. The Romans, Jews and Arabs all have documents referring to gonorrhea and each society had their own description of symptoms and treatment. The origins of Gonorrhea can be traced back to the Old Testament in the books of Leviticus and Genesis where it is described as a contagious, continuous passing out of semen and painful erection. The disease has been referred to by various names in different societies. The Roman physician Galen in 130 AD described the disease as an "involuntary escape of semen".   Hippocrates (460–375 BC) called it "Strangury." The disease was also referred to as “The Clap,” referring either to the “clapping” feeling experienced by the infected person during urination. In those days, men were considered victims and women as the source of the disease. The organism that causes the disease was first discovered in 1879 in Albert Neisser, hence the organisma being called Neisseria Gonorrhoea. The word Gonorrhea itself derives from the Greek, meaning "the flow of seed" (gono: seed, rhea: flow).

TargetRLF1: The best way to protect yourself from HIV is to abstain from having sex. TargetRLF1: A chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary infection test detects the bacteria that cause chlamydia, a sexually transmitted infection (STI). This test tells you if you have the chlamydia infection so that you can get treat treatment and avoid complications. Your provider takes a sample of urine or secretions from your vagina, penis or rectum and sends it to a lab for testing using a test called NAATs (short for nucleic acid amplification tests), which is very accurate and easy to take. Results are usually ready in a day. Less often, providers use a cell culture to test for chlamydia. In this case, your provider swabs your vagina, urethra or anus to collect a sample of fluid and cells. They send the cells to a lab to test. If bacteria are present, they will grow, which indicates an infection. Providers may choose this type of chlamydia test to detect infections in the anus. Or they may use a cell culture to see how chlamydia treatments are working. Because of the possibility of multiple sexually transmitted infections, all patients with any sexually transmitted disease (STD) should be evaluated for chlamydial infection. There are no risks involved with these tests. Rarely the results of the tests may be inconclusive or unclear and you may need to give another sample.

TargetRLF1: Uncomplicated Chlamydia also known as Chlamydial Genitourinary infection can be easily cured with antibiotics and is usually resolved within days or weeks. It is important that you take all of the medicine your healthcare provider gives you because untreated or improperly treated Chlamydia can lead to serious complications. Do not share medicine for chlamydia with anyone. Recommended drugs include Doxycylcine, Azithromycin, Levofloxacin and Amoxicillin. When taken properly, drugs will stop the infection and could decrease your chances of having problems later. Although medicine will stop the infection, it will not undo any permanent damage caused by the disease. You should notify your sexual partner(s) to get tested and treated, if necessary. Repeat infection with chlamydia is common. You should receive testing again about three months after your treatment, even if your sex partner(s) receives treatment. If the mother had a documented chlamydial infection during pregnancy that was untreated, presumptively treat the infant, even without confirmation of infection.
